items.myth: Unsupervised learning automatically finds the 'true' groups in data.
items.reality: Algorithms find structure relative to their objective and assumptions. Multiple valid partitions can exist; 'truth' requires domain validation, not just running clustering.
items.example: The same customer dataset can yield lifestyle segments or geographic segments depending on features included—neither is uniquely 'correct' without business context.

items.myth: Unsupervised learning is unsupervised because the model needs no training.
items.reality: Models still fit parameters on training data; 'unsupervised' means no external label column y, not that learning is absent.
items.example: k-means iteratively updates centroids on training points—heavy optimization with no y column, but still trained.

items.myth: Self-supervised learning is just another name for unsupervised learning.
items.reality: Self-supervised learning constructs surrogate labels from data (mask prediction, contrastive pairs). Classical unsupervised structure discovery (clustering, PCA) does not require such pseudo-targets.
items.example: BERT-style masked language modeling is self-supervised; k-means on user embeddings is unsupervised—they solve different problems.
